claim.hypothesis: One-stage tournaments are behaviorally different from multistage setups
claim.hypothesis_location: discussed in the abstract and introduction (specifically, p 150)
claim.statement: Average effort inthe first stage of the two-stage treatment is 84.8 (median = 84). Thus, subjects behave much more competitively in the multistage tournament, exerting efforts that are almost 20% higher than those of their counterparts in the one-stage treatment (average effort = 71.7, median = 75). A Mann-Whitney U-test confirms that the treatment difference is statistically significant (|z| = 2.536, p = .011, two sided)
claim.statement_location: section IV. Reults, B. Testing Behavioral Equivalence (p. 162) and Table2 (p. 160)

data.wave_or_subset: participant-stage observations from first stages of the main treatment (one-stage and two-stage tournaments)
data.notes: one-shot between subject design (subjects participated in only one of the treatments), no repetition, no learning/training phase

method.description: The paper studies incentives in multistage elimination tournaments using a controlled lab experiment. The authors run two main treatments where subjects take part in a two-stage tournament or in one-stage tournament. It allows the authors to compare how much effort people exert in these two settings.
method.steps:
1. Program the experiment in z-Tree: participants will be randomly assigned to a 
- two-stage tournament (TS - four participants compete for promotion to a second stage and promotion depends on their output. After stage 1, two subjects with the lowest output levels will be eliminated and receive a low wage, and two subjects with the highest output levels are promoted and compete again in stage 2. Only stage 1 is analysed for the focal result.
or
- one-stage tournament (OS four subjects compete once for two top positions. The wage structure is calibrated so that OS is strategically equivalent to the first stage of TS: promoted subjects OS earn the sum of the intermediate wage in TS and the monetary equivalent of the second-stage option value so that equilibrium effort is the same in both treatments.)
2. Recruit participants using ORSEE. 
3. Upon arrival, seat them in separate cubicles to ensure anonymity and prevent communication and provide with written instructions specific to their treatment. Instructions must be neutrally framed and avoid terms such as “tournament,” “final,” or “winner.”
4. Provide a cost table for all effort levels. 
5. Require participants to complete several control questions (not specified in the article). Start the experiment only after all answers are correct.
6. In each stage, participants simultaneously choose an effort level between 0 and 125. Higher effort means higher costs, with costs rising more than proportionally. A participant’s performance depended on and a random component representing luck (the software adds a random number to the effort) to simulate a combination of effort and luck. 
7. The whole experimental session lasted on average 100 minutes. In the two main treatments, subjects earned an average of €18.75 (€1 ≈ US$1.25 at the time of experiment), including a showup fee of €4 and a fixed payment of €3 for completing the postexperimental questionnaire. In the tournament, earnings ranged between €0.15 and €18.04.
8. Extract effort choices of all OS participants stage 1 TS participants. 
9. Calculate median and average effort and compare them using a two-sided Mann–Whitney U-test.
method.independent_variables: Type of tournament (one-stage vs two stage)
method.tools_software: z-Tree (for conducting and programming the experiment), ORSEE (for recruiting participants)

results.summary: Participants made more effort in multistage tournament (almost 20% more than participants in the one-stage treatment). Average effort in the first stage of the two-stage (TS) treatment was 84.8 (median = 84) whereas in the one-stage OS) treatment average effort was 71.7 (median = 75). A Mann-Whitney U-test confirmed that the difference is statistically significant (|z| = 2.536, p = .011, two sided)

metadata.original_paper_title: Promotions and Incentives: The Case of Multistage Elimination Tournaments.
